narrative: I'm sorry to share this news, but the admissions model predicted a 57.3% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first try, which led to the rejection of your law school application. Your profile includes being in the third decile for grades in your first and third years of school—lower than the typical student's fifth or sixth decile—along with an LSAT score of 34, below the average of about 37, and an undergraduate GPA of 2.8, compared to the usual 3.2. You're from a middle-income family, identify as male and White, and plan to attend full-time, which aligns with most applicants. The model weighed these details against patterns from thousands of past students to forecast bar exam success. The strongest influence on pushing your prediction toward failure was your third-year decile rank, followed closely by your first-year decile, then your LSAT score. Your family income level and undergraduate GPA came next in influence, each nudging the outlook slightly more toward failure, with your racial background, gender, and full-time status having the smallest impacts, still leaning that way. Overall, your academic deciles and test scores stood out as bigger drags compared to typical students, whose stronger early performances often predict bar passage. While no single factor doomed your application, these lower-than-average markers in school performance and entrance exams tipped the scales. To boost your future chances, focus on realistic steps like retaking the LSAT to aim higher, strengthening your GPA through additional coursework or post-bac programs, and building a track record of academic improvement. Demonstrating upward trends in grades could help offset those early deciles in a reapplication. Many students in similar spots have succeeded by targeting these areas before reapplying. Hang in there—this is a data-driven hurdle, but your path forward is in your hands.
